hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_desc: A review meeting with Ana's manager that consolidates Block 1 (weeks 1-7): key QA vocabulary and the tenses and strategies from the whole block -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 8 -- Checkpoint & Review of Block 1
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 8 -- Checkpoint & Review
lesson.grammar_point: review: Block 1 tenses and communication strategies (checkpoint)

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to put new code on a server so people can use it
an important point or achievement in a project
the steps a task goes through from start to finish
to decide what is most important to do first
the real reason a problem happens
something that stops you from making progress

paras: After seven weeks on the remote QA team, Ana had her first review meeting with Marcus, her manager. She was a little nervous, but she was also ready. She had prepared a short summary of her work, and she wanted to show how much she had learned. Marcus opened the call with a simple question: 'So, Ana, tell me about your first block. What are you proud of, and what was hard?'

Ana talked about her milestone: in week five she had tested a difficult feature and helped the team deploy it on time. She explained her workflow: every morning she checks the backlog, and she has learned to prioritize the bugs by severity. When a test fails, she does not panic; she looks for the root cause and writes a clear bug report. Her biggest challenge, she said, was a blocker in week three, when a broken environment stopped her work for a day. She did not stay silent: she raised it in the stand-up and followed up until it was fixed.

Marcus was happy with her progress. He gave her one piece of advice for the next block: 'When a requirement is not clear, always clarify it before you start, and think about the edge cases early.' Ana agreed. She had grown from someone who was afraid to speak on a call into an engineer who asks good questions, gives clear updates, and follows up. Block one was complete, and she was ready for block two.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 checkpoint reading, review of weeks 1-7

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Ana was fired after seven weeks because she made too many mistakes.
In her first review, Ana shows her progress across Block 1: her milestone, her workflow, how she handles bugs and blockers, and how she communicates.
The text is a technical manual about how to deploy software to a server.

items: Ana came to the review meeting with no preparation at all.
The text says the opposite: 'She had prepared a short summary of her work.'
Ana learned to prioritize the bugs by their severity.
The text says: 'she has learned to prioritize the bugs by severity.'
When a blocker stopped her work, Ana stayed silent and did nothing.
The text says she raised it in the stand-up and followed up until it was fixed.
Marcus advised Ana to clarify unclear requirements and think about edge cases early.
The text says: 'always clarify it before you start, and think about the edge cases early.'

gapfill.text: Remember the Block 1 tenses: present perfect for experience ('she has learned'), past simple for a finished event ('she tested a feature in week five'), and present simple for a routine ('every morning she checks the backlog').

bank.label: Useful sentences for a review meeting
items: In week five, I tested a difficult feature and helped the team deploy it.
Every morning I check the backlog and prioritize the bugs by severity.
When a test fails, I look for the root cause before I write the report.
My biggest challenge was a blocker in week three.
I raised it in the stand-up and followed up until it was fixed.
When a requirement is not clear, I clarify it before I start.
I always think about the edge cases early.
I am proud of my progress, and I am ready for the next block.

lf.title: Which tense fits the review?
items: to prioritize by severity. (experience so far)
on a new edge case. (in progress)

lf.text: Use past simple for a finished moment in time (week five), present perfect for experience up to now (I have learned), present simple for a routine (every morning), and present continuous for something in progress right now.

items.situation: In your review, tell your manager about one thing you have achieved in the last weeks. Use the present perfect.
tips: I have tested three new features this block.
I have learned to write clearer bug reports.

items.situation: Describe your daily routine as a QA engineer in one sentence. Use the present simple.
tips: Every morning I check the backlog and prioritize the bugs by severity.
I test the new features and I write a bug report for each problem.

items.situation: Talk about a finished challenge from a past week. Use the past simple.
tips: Last month a blocker stopped my work, but I raised it in the stand-up.
In week three I found the root cause of an intermittent bug.

items.situation: Your manager gives you an unclear requirement. Ask a clarifying question before you start.
tips: Could you clarify what you mean by this requirement?
So you are saying the feature must also work offline, right?

items: Give a 30-second summary of your first block. Use three of the review words: milestone, workflow, challenge. Speak from your notes.
Now talk about a bug you handled: name its severity, the root cause, and how you followed up. Use past simple and present perfect.
Answer your manager's question, 'What are you proud of, and what was hard?' in your own words. No notes.
Your manager asks about an unclear requirement. Clarify it first, then explain how you would think about the edge cases.

answerkey.title: Reveal the Block 1 toolkit
list: Vocabulary: requirement, to deploy, milestone, workflow, to prioritize, severity, root cause, blocker, to follow up, to clarify, edge case, challenge.
Present perfect for experience: 'I have tested three features this block.'
Past simple for a finished event: 'In week five, I tested a difficult feature.'
Present simple for a routine: 'Every morning I check the backlog.'
Communication: give clear updates in the stand-up, clarify what is not clear, and follow up until it is fixed.
answerkey.note: This is your Block 1 toolkit. Everything here comes back in Block 2, so keep it close.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, this is Marcus. I want to leave a quick note after our review call, so you have it in writing. First, congratulations on a strong first block. Your milestone in week five was excellent, and the way you found the root cause of that intermittent bug really helped the team. I also liked how you handled the blocker in week three; you did not stay silent, you raised it and you followed up. For the next block, my advice is simple. When a requirement is not clear, clarify it before you start, and think about the edge cases early. That will save you time. Keep up the good work, and let us talk again in two weeks.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. I heard your review went well, and I am not surprised. Here is a short recap of what you have built in Block 1. You have learned the words of QA, from severity to root cause. You can give a clear stand-up update, you can read a bug report for the detail, and you can follow a colleague with a different accent. Most important, you have learned to speak up: to prioritize, to clarify, and to follow up. In Block 2, we go further. We will talk about requirements, passive voice for reporting problems, and conditionals for cause and effect. But the base is here, and it is strong. Well done, Ana.

extra_audio.text: First, Ana prepared a short summary before her review meeting with Marcus. Then she talked about her milestone in week five, when she helped deploy a difficult feature. Next, she explained her workflow and how she prioritizes bugs by severity. After that, she described her biggest challenge, a blocker in week three, and how she followed up until it was fixed. Finally, Marcus advised her to clarify unclear requirements and think about the edge cases early.
